speaker: interviewer
text: Tape number 5. Mrs. Kahane, just before the break, we were talking about the education that you have-- you have had in Australia. Would you like to mention more about that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I was talking about being the best country in the world, a country who-- [SOBS] who offered me refuge. Nobody can make up for what I've lost. But I became a human being, standing shoulder to shoulder with everyone else. I was able to get-- to matriculate in this country, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] to get a diploma in journalism, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] to work as a journalist for 11 years-- with the Australian Jewish News, from 1970 to 1981-- because I got myself into a course where the prerequisite for the course was you had to be attached to the press. Then, the ghetto girl went to investigate. And she got herself a BA from the Melbourne University. What a beautiful country this is. I know I worked hard. But I was on a scholarship, right through. For my master's, I did a research thesis on the survival of German Jews-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] in-- on the survival of German Jews, a 40,000 word thesis. And I received my master's. And I'm currently working, transforming that into a book, which I-- the title of the book is, or will be when it's published, I wanted to bring in the rescuers because of my own personal experience. And I think the-- most academics and researchers have been preoccupied with the horrors of the war, not just with the goodness in it, even though it was rare. But this American rabbi [NON-ENGLISH] said, 'The Nazis the ripped out the heart of humanity. And there were traces of humanness.' and I would like to trace that in my work.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mrs. Kahane, why did you want to give this interview?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I feel that [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I am 70, comparatively, a young survivor. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] Being young didn't always help me. It didn't help me with German restitution. I accused the Germans for not granting me restitution, even though my papers were black and white. They thought I would probably benefit from their pensions for too long, because I was too young when I applied for it. But seeing I got away from that liquidation center, I will tell them that there must be lots of Nazis running this scheme.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mrs. Kahane, thank you very much. Mrs. Kahane, what can you tell me about this photo?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I have, I have friends who went to Poland recently. I have given them the address because this photo-- this is a photo of the property and residence of my uncle and aunt, whose name is Ehrlich, E-H-R-L-I-C-H, Laib and Menucha Ehrlich. They had a family of five daughters and two sons. One of these sons is the only survivor of my extended family. He's a cousin-- he is no longer. He died in Israel. But his daughter lives in Israel. And she always wanted to know something about her roots. So I wrote to her, sent her the photo, which was taken by friends of mine who visited Lwow. And I have given them instructions just to photograph the house, in case this girl has a chance, in future, perhaps to claim, because her grandparents were very wealthy people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the address of this house?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, the address is at the back of the photo. And it's number 33 Zrodlan Street, Z-R-O-D-L-A-N. But the number could be extended, I think, 33 to something. But I don't know it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mrs. Kahane, what can you me tell me about this photo?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, actually, I told you a little bit about it before. I completed a course of a lab technician before I came out. I wanted a skill that could-- I could be utilized, could be utilized in Australia. And I worked for the Brisbane Blood Bank. Brisbane Blood Bank is in the main part of the city, called Edward Street. And I was promoted in an unofficial way at the beginning, because I tried-- tried to translate some forms for the chief doctor. And then he, he asked me, would I be able to speak to a group of migrants who arrived-- would I be able to speak to them in German. Not all were German. There were some Poles. But they came from Germany, could understand it. And I had to bring across-- to communicate to them that in Australia, unlike in Europe, people donate blood. They don't-- they're not paid for blood. I know some students in Germany, for example, they are hard up. And they gave their blood for money. Whereas this is the difference in Australia. And we tried to show them, at this migrant camp, that migrants-- at that time at least, migration was fairly numerous, migration to Australia-- that they were recipients of blood. But they were not donors. And apparently, this is-- this attests to the fact that my appeal to this group of migrants was-- had some impact. Because we had people coming to the blood bank, doing their duty, and donating their blood. And this is just a photo, which appeared also in the at the time. And people were really following their conscious, that they weren't just greedy recipients of blood, but they gave it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what year was this photo taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think this probably would be 1950 or beginning '51.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who's this in the photo, Mrs. Kahane?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's myself, just after the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was it taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was taken-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I'm not sure. It could be Berlin. It could be Munich.

speaker: interviewer
text: What can you tell me about this photo?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, this photo is, perhaps only important in placing it in time. I was looking-- I was interested in humanities, actually. But I, I was asked-- advised to get some skill that wouldn't take too long in training. And I chose to train in a hospital, as a lab technician, to be able to get a certificate when I came to Australia, that I could sort of lean back on. And it proved a good-- it stood me by, actually, with my next job. And I'll tell you a little bit. I told you about it with the-- with the people that I appealed to volunteer to give their blood. And that was a consequence, actually, of this.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was this taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was taken in Munich, at a-- it was a Jewish-American sponsored hospital. And it was in Munich, in [GERMAN].

speaker: interviewer
text: And what year would this have been?

speaker: interviewee
text: And that would have been 1947.

speaker: interviewer
text: What can you tell me about this photo?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is our wedding photo. We are married in Munich, on the 13th of May-- 1-3, May 1949. We've been married for 47 years. This is my husband, Jacob, who is also a sole survivor. It's a lonely photo of two lonely people. [SOBS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you meet your husband?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I was in search for relatives everywhere. He worked at [NON-ENGLISH] in the accountancy. And I was looking for a cousin who happened to be a distant cousin. I never knew him before. We met at kid-- as kids, sometimes, at someone else's wedding. And he worked in the same building. And this is where I met him. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we came out together to Australia.

speaker: interviewer
text: What can you tell me about this photo?

speaker: interviewee
text: This photo is my, my graduation-- from my graduation for a master's degree in Melbourne at Monash University. I did a research thesis. The title of the thesis was And I wish to stop here for a minute, to pay tribute to my husband, who was with me in the photo, without whose help and encouragement, I would never have been able to complete my studies. [SOBS] He was always behind me. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] [SOBS] Excused me for the late dinners, for unprepared meals. One Friday, I went to the fridge, to find fish. We had the challah on the table, but there was no fish. I forgot to buy it. I had to do an essay. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Thank you, Papa.

speaker: interviewer
text: What's this book?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, this book is my [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] thesis, which [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] provided me with the master's degree. It's And it concentrates-- again, I was motivated by my own survival. And it focuses a great deal on [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] not just the Jews, but I have a-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I did a portrait of the rescuers who helped them.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year did you do your thesis?

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long did it take you?

speaker: interviewee
text: It, it took me nearly four years. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And it's had very good-- it had an impact on people who have read it and who encouraged me, particularly, since [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Daniel Goldhagen's book came out, on the [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] Now to make-- to transform this into a book. Because [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] he is right in his way, saying that not just the trained SS, but the ordinary rabble were behind the executions. But there were rare, good, decent Germans. And I would like to focus on them, to say that there was-- otherwise, it's a very dark world.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you want to tell me about this book?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, actually, the book isn't mine. I have a contribution in this book. I have shown the paper, which I gave at a conference, to a German professor who [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] edits the And this comes out once a year.

speaker: interviewer
text: It's mostly about people who survived in camps. I wanted-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Professor Benz wrote to me, would I expand on this? And the article in it is called [SPEAKING GERMAN], which means a Polish Jewess survived in Germany.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I, I might as well add here, you asked me why I wanted to give this interview. Because people who have not been in camps have frequently been hiding in places where, apart from a few piles of straw, they hadn't seen anything. I have lived as a human being. I have fought. I fended for myself. I wanted to show the kind of life that an individual who has not been in the camp. Because we know, we are aware of the victims of the camp and even survivors of the camp numerically. But they can't-- either some are not in the position to communicate what they went through, and people tend to put them under the same rubric as a survivor of Auschwitz or as a survivor of Dachau. Well, I am a different kind of survivor. I don't know where I got the strength. And I can't explain to any of those people [SOBS] who will ever see this why it was up to me to survive. But I did. And I would like them to know all the contrasts and all the obstacles that a young girl went through, that we Jews have an extra strength. And perhaps, it's shown in this.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mrs. Kahane, thank you very much.
